title: Evangelion as shonen
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion as shonen             Quote Post by GAP » Mon Nov 26, 2012 4:04 pm What amazes that Evangelion for all its darkness, character drama and psychological themes, that it is still classified as a shonen work. My experience with shonen is mostly that you usually have some sort hotblooded hero and his group of friends and they go off to fight monsters and generally save the world from an powerful evil. Evangelion does do these things but it doesn't feel like a shonen work and it doesn't use the tropes in the traditional manner. For one thing, it seems to be more about the characters rather than the storyline, it analyzes itself and its even its own fandom. Shinji, Rei and Asuka roles have been more or less reversed. It feels more like a seinen rather than a shonen but I could wrong. Does Evangelion work as a shonen? It doesn't feel that way to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Nov 26, 2012 4:09 pm The manga was originally serialized in Monthly Shonen Ace ; it moved to the seinen Young Ace in the last few years.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Wed Nov 28, 2012 6:35 pm It was technically a shonen by sheer virtue of being in that magazine but it idnd't like like any shonen I read. Maybe I should read less shonen but Shinji wasn't the typical shonen hero that I see in most manga.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Wed Nov 28, 2012 8:33 pm Don't know much about anime or the making of NGE, but as i understand it Anno intentionally pitched it as a shonen to hook in unwitting viewers to much more mature themes and elements. And while i'm not sure of this either, i think NGE had a lot of deconstruction of typical shonen tropes, another reason for wanting to present it as shonen i guess. I see NGE as a seinen maskerading as shonen.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Sun Jan 06, 2013 12:33 am Well, the Action Arc was certainly shonen. It was full of action, angels, and lightheartedness. In short, sickening. I'm not sure how the manga is qualified to represent the TV series, though.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sun Jan 06, 2013 2:47 am ^ What do the rules have to do with anything?

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Sun Jan 06, 2013 11:18 am It's Evangelion. The rules stipulate that unless there is fanservice, it should be dull, depressing, psychological, and gorey. Unless, of course, they are juxtaposing the fanservice over emotional trauma. Then it's okay.

[]: Re: Evangelion as shonen             Quote Post by pat457 » Tue Jan 08, 2013 8:49 am GAP wrote: What amazes that Evangelion for all its darkness, character drama and psychological themes, that it is still classified as a shonen work. My experience with shonen is mostly that you usually have some sort hotblooded hero and his group of friends and they go off to fight monsters and generally save the world from an powerful evil. Evangelion does do these things but it doesn't feel like a shonen work and it doesn't use the tropes in the traditional manner. For one thing, it seems to be more about the characters rather than the storyline, it analyzes itself and its even its own fandom. Shinji, Rei and Asuka roles have been more or less reversed. It feels more like a seinen rather than a shonen but I could wrong. Does Evangelion work as a shonen? It doesn't feel that way to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Tue Jan 08, 2013 10:17 am I suppose it really all depends on which episode you're looking at. Some of the content from the beginning of the show was nearly nothing but action, whereas episodes 20-26 were pretty damn introspective.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Tue Jan 08, 2013 9:56 pm Nuclear Lunchbox wrote: I suppose it really all depends on which episode you're looking at. Some of the content from the beginning of the show was nearly nothing but action , whereas episodes 20-26 were pretty damn introspective.

[]:             Quote Post by Nyanlathotep » Thu Jan 10, 2013 2:15 pm GAP wrote: It was technically a shonen by sheer virtue of being in that magazine but it idnd't like like any shonen I read. Maybe I should read less shonen but Shinji wasn't the typical shonen hero that I see in most manga.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Jan 10, 2013 4:42 pm Maybe that classification refers more to art style and genre conventions/setting? The setup itself is pretty classic: Unlikely hero must fight monsters to save planet, meets pretty girls along the way and... no, actually, it doesn't do what you want it to. As for "classic shonen protagonist", I always felt that Touji and and Kensuke (who is the spitting image of Anno's previous protagonist "Jean" anyway) looked like the two most common Shonen protagonist archetypes - Touji is the "tough guy with a protector's instinct/sense of justice" (Most triumphant example: Kurosaki Ichigo. Don't touch his little sisters!) while Kensuke is the archetypical "big dreamer" (Popular example: Uzumaki Naruto. He wants to be Hokage!) . But Kensuke doesn't get chosen and Touji doesn't get heroic beginner's luck, because you see, this time the kid with the influential parents didn't just happen to have a classic heroic personality. Asuka is pretty much your basic rival character, for once not being the same sex as the protagonist. They're usually male, but it's more because the protagonists usually are. Sailor moon has the other Rei and later Haruka, Claymore has Helen and Ophelia etc...
